answer: In the runic signature passage of Elene (paragraphs p-d2e607 and p-8f8a1b, cynewulf-elene-d2d132 ch.1), Cynewulf reveals several things by embedding his name in runes (spelling C-Y-N-E-W-U-L-F) in the poem's epilogue: (1) His identity — the runes encode his name so future readers will know who composed the poem and can pray for his soul; (2) His personal spiritual biography — he confesses he was once 'stained by my deeds, shackled by sins, worn down by sorrows, bitterly bound, beset by troubles'; (3) That God granted him the gift of poetry in old age — 'the mighty King granted me learning through a luminous form... unbound my bone-house, unwound my breast-lock, unlocked the art of song'; (4) His advanced age at composition — he calls himself 'old and ready for my journey' writing 'through this frail house'; (5) His former worldly life — he once received treasures in the mead-hall, rode a horse, and enjoyed the joys of youth, all now faded; (6) The transience of earthly life — 'Joy has waned with the years, youth is gone... life's joys have slipped by as water glides'; and (7) An implicit plea for intercessory prayer — by naming himself in runes he invites readers to remember him and pray for his soul at the Last Judgment, which the following passage (p-dcbbfe) describes in detail.
